title: PDS 70 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
ALMA image of the young planetary system PDS 70, located about 400 light-years from Earth. The image shows a circumstellar disk where planets are forming, including the confirmed planets PDS 70b and PDS 70c embedded within the disk.


Figure 1: Credit: ALMA (ESO/NAOJ/NRAO) / Balsalobre-Ruza et al.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Figure 1: High-resolution SPHERE image of the dusty disk around the young star IM Lupi, revealing fine structures in scattered light. Credit: ESO/H. Avenhaus et al./DARTT-S collaboration.
As shown in Figure 1, high-resolution scattered-light observations reveal the dusty disk surrounding the young star IM Lup.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Object names



Catalog
Name




Primary name
IM Lup


Other designations

Region
Lupus 2 cloud

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
IM Lup is associated with the Lupus 2 cloud and lies at a distance of 161 ± 10 pc (Cleeves et al. 2016). Its location in a nearby star-forming region and its young age make it a useful system for studying how gas and dust evolve differently during the early stages of protoplanetary disk evolution.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Overview of the system
IM Lup is a young, massive, and extended protoplanetary disk whose gas and dust components show markedly different spatial distributions (Cleeves et al. 2016). ALMA observations of the continuum, \(^{12}\)CO, \(^{13}\)CO, and C\(^{18}\)O were used together with physical and chemical modeling to constrain the structure of the disk.
A key result of the paper is that the millimeter dust disk is truncated at about 313 au, while the gas disk extends to at least 570 au and perhaps as far as 970 au (Cleeves et al. 2016). This contrast indicates that large grains are more radially compact than the gas, consistent with dust growth, settling, and radial drift.
The study also found tentative evidence for weak ring-like substructure in the millimeter continuum and showed that the disk is both massive and flared, properties that may be related to its young age (Cleeves et al. 2016).

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
ALMA observations show that IM Lup has a two-component millimeter dust structure: a bright inner disk and a broad outer dust halo truncated at 313 au (Cleeves et al. 2016). In contrast, the gas traced by CO isotopologues is much more extended and appears smoother in radial distribution.
The continuum data also show shallow deviations from a smooth radial profile, suggesting tentative ring-like substructure. These features are weak and were not resolved as sharply as the rings later seen in higher-resolution disk surveys, but they are still morphologically important because they hint that substructure may already be present in IM Lup (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The paper describes these features as small breaks in the deprojected continuum brightness profile near 150 au and 250 au.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
Dust in IM Lup was observed with ALMA continuum data at 1.3 mm and 875 μm (Cleeves et al. 2016). These observations trace the distribution of large, millimeter-sized grains, which are more concentrated than the gas and appear sharply truncated at 313 au.
The continuum emission is dominated by a bright central component plus a broad outer dust distribution. The paper models the large-grain dust disk as being more radially compact and more vertically settled than the gas. This is consistent with the idea that large grains have already evolved significantly through growth, settling toward the midplane, and radial drift (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The inner dust disk is also inferred to be optically thick, which helps explain the reduced line emission seen near the center in some CO isotopologue maps.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The gas component of IM Lup was observed in \(^{12}\)CO, \(^{13}\)CO, and C\(^{18}\)O transitions (Cleeves et al. 2016). These lines show that the gas disk is much more extended than the millimeter dust disk.
The paper finds that the gas disk extends to at least 570 au and possibly to ~970 au, making IM Lup one of the clearest examples of a large dust–gas size difference in a protoplanetary disk (Cleeves et al. 2016).
The gas structure appears relatively smooth compared with the dust continuum. In addition, the central low-velocity channels of \(^{12}\)CO J = 2–1 show a diffuse outer component or halo, which may be associated with extended gas beyond the main disk.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
At this stage, Cleeves et al. (2016) do not provide direct evidence for embedded protoplanets in IM Lup. However, the paper does identify processes that are strongly connected to early planet formation.
The strong difference between the compact millimeter dust disk and the much larger gas disk is consistent with grain growth, settling, and radial drift, which are key steps in the evolution from dust grains to planetesimals (Cleeves et al. 2016). The tentative ring-like breaks at ~150 au and ~250 au are also morphologically interesting, although the authors emphasize that higher-resolution observations are needed to confirm them.
For now, this paper provides indirect evidence of dust evolution relevant to planet formation, rather than direct evidence of planets opening gaps.

title: IM Lup — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of IM Lup
Cleeves et al. (2016) interpret IM Lup as a young and massive disk in which gas and dust are already evolving differently (Cleeves et al. 2016). The large grains are more compact radially and more settled vertically than the gas, while the gas remains extended far into the outer disk.
The study concludes that the dust structure can be understood as a bright inner disk plus a broad outer halo truncated at 313 au, whereas the gas extends to much larger radii. This contrast supports a picture in which the solid component is undergoing substantial evolution, even though the system is still very young.
The paper also suggests that the disk may retain some surrounding diffuse material, since the low-velocity \(^{12}\)CO emission includes a halo-like component that could be related to a remnant envelope, captured material, or other extended gas structures. Even so, the main morphological result remains the same: IM Lup shows a clear decoupling between the spatial distributions of gas and millimeter dust (Cleeves et al. 2016).

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Figure 1: ALMA image of the protoplanetary disk around HD 163296 showing prominent ring–gap substructures in the dust continuum. The disk exhibits several dust rings and gaps associated with pressure structures in the disk material. Image credit: ALMA (ESO/NAOJ/NRAO).
As shown in Figure 1, the HD 163296 disk exhibits multiple dark gaps and bright rings in the millimeter dust continuum emission.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Object names



Catalog
Name




Primary name
HD 163296


Other designations

Region
Serpens star-forming region

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
HD 163296 is a young Herbig Ae star located at a distance of approximately 101–122 parsecs (~330–400 light-years) from Earth (Isella et al. 2016; Rab et al. 2020).
The star belongs to the class of intermediate-mass pre–main sequence stars and is surrounded by one of the most extensively studied protoplanetary disks. Because the disk is large, bright, and relatively isolated, it provides an ideal laboratory for investigating the processes that shape planet-forming disks.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Overview of the system
HD 163296 is one of the best-studied protoplanetary disks with resolved substructures. High-resolution ALMA observations revealed a system of rings and gaps in the millimeter dust continuum emission at radii of roughly 60 AU, 100 AU, and 160 AU (Isella et al. 2016).
Subsequent studies showed that the gas disk exhibits a more complex structure. Thermo-chemical modeling demonstrated that some radial features observed in CO emission are strongly influenced by temperature gradients and dust opacity effects rather than solely by changes in gas density (Rab et al. 2020).
In addition, high-resolution spectroscopic observations have revealed localized deviations from Keplerian rotation, which are interpreted as evidence for embedded planets perturbing the gas velocity field within the disk (pinte2020?).
Together, these results indicate that HD 163296 is a key system for studying the interaction between forming planets and their natal disks.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
ALMA observations show that the disk of HD 163296 contains a radial sequence of bright rings and dark gaps in the dust continuum emission (Isella et al. 2016).
The three most prominent dust gaps are located at approximately:

~60 AU
~100 AU
~160 AU

These substructures likely correspond to regions where the surface density of solid material has been reduced.
The dust rings surrounding the gaps are interpreted as pressure maxima, where millimeter-sized particles accumulate as a consequence of radial drift and particle trapping within the disk.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
Dust in protoplanetary disks emits thermal radiation at millimeter wavelengths. ALMA observations at 1.3 mm revealed that the dust distribution in HD 163296 contains several radial depletions associated with the gaps (Isella et al. 2016).
Modeling of the continuum emission indicates that the dust surface density drops dramatically inside the gaps, with depletion factors reaching:

>100 in the inner gap

~70 in the middle gap

~6 in the outer gap

These results suggest that solid particles are strongly concentrated in the rings, where pressure maxima trap drifting dust grains.
Dust rings therefore represent locations where conditions may favor the formation of planetesimals, the building blocks of planets.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The gas disk in HD 163296 has been observed through molecular emission lines such as ¹²CO, ¹³CO, and C¹⁸O (Isella et al. 2016; Rab et al. 2020).
Unlike the dust, the gas emission is more extended and does not display equally sharp ringed structures. The gas disk extends out to approximately 550 AU.
Thermo-chemical modeling has shown that some radial intensity features observed in ¹²CO J = 2–1 are caused by radiative transfer effects rather than true gas density variations. In particular, emission from the back side of the disk can be partially absorbed by optically thick dust rings, producing apparent peaks in the radial intensity profile (Rab et al. 2020).
This demonstrates that interpreting gas observations requires detailed modeling of both the dust distribution and the disk temperature structure.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
Several lines of evidence suggest that HD 163296 hosts forming planets.
Hydrodynamic simulations indicate that the gaps in the dust disk can be reproduced by embedded planets with masses comparable to those of Saturn or Jupiter (Isella et al. 2016).
In addition, measurements of the gas velocity field reveal localized deviations from Keplerian rotation, which are interpreted as perturbations produced by planets embedded within the disk (pinte2020?).
The inferred properties of these candidate planets are approximately:



Radius (AU)
Estimated mass




~100 AU
~1 MJ


~165 AU
~1.3 MJ



These measurements represent one of the first cases in which forming planets have been inferred from direct perturbations in the gas velocity field of a protoplanetary disk.

title: HD 163296 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of HD 163296
The structure of the HD 163296 disk is likely shaped by a combination of physical processes.
The presence of deep dust gaps and pressure maxima suggests that planet–disk interaction plays a major role in sculpting the disk morphology.
However, thermo-chemical modeling shows that the interpretation of gas emission lines is complicated by temperature gradients and radiative transfer effects within the disk (Rab et al. 2020).
Together, the observations of dust rings, gas dynamics, and velocity perturbations indicate that HD 163296 is a system in which planet formation is actively reshaping the disk structure.

As shown in Figure 1, the AS 209 disk exhibits a sequence of bright rings and dark gaps in the dust continuum emission.

text:

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
AS 209 is located in the Ophiuchus molecular cloud, a nearby star-forming region that hosts many young stellar systems. In the literature, the source is described as a T Tauri star in the young Ophiuchus star-forming region at a distance of 126 pc from the Sun (Fedele et al. 2018).
Its relative proximity makes AS 209 an excellent target for high-resolution millimeter observations, allowing both the dust and gas substructures of the disk to be studied in detail.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Overview of the system

“The formation of planets occurs within protoplanetary disks surrounding young stars, resulting in perturbation of the gas and dust surface densities.”

AS 209 is a protoplanetary disk with a remarkably structured radial morphology. Early ALMA continuum observations showed that the dust emission is organized into a central component plus two prominent rings, separated by two clear dark gaps (Fedele et al. 2018). In particular, the 1.3 mm continuum profile was described as having rings at 75 AU and 130 AU, with gaps at 62 AU and 103 AU (Fedele et al. 2018).
Later high-resolution observations revealed that the disk is even more complex, with additional substructures at smaller and intermediate radii. In the DSHARP analysis, AS 209 was highlighted as a system with many gaps, reinforcing its importance as a benchmark object for studying disk substructures and their physical origins (Zhang et al. 2018).
Molecular-line observations also show that the gas disk is structured. In particular, the optically thin C\(^{18}\)O emission displays a compact central component within ~60 AU and an outer gas ring peaking near 140 AU, while thermo-chemical modeling indicates that the gas surface density is reduced in radial intervals associated with the dust gaps (Favre et al. 2019). Taken together, these observations make AS 209 one of the clearest examples of a disk in which both dust and gas are dynamically perturbed.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
ALMA observations show that the disk of AS 209 contains a structured radial pattern of bright rings and dark gaps in the millimeter dust continuum emission (Fedele et al. 2018). At the angular resolution of the early 1.3 mm data, the most prominent substructures are two bright rings at 75 AU and 130 AU, separated by two dust gaps at 62 AU and 103 AU.
Gas observations reveal that the disk morphology is not limited to the dust component. The C\(^{18}\)O emission follows a related but not identical radial structure, with a central peak, a deficit between the two main dust gaps, and an outer gas ring around 140 AU (Favre et al. 2019). Thermochemical modeling further suggests that the gas surface density is reduced in two broad radial intervals, approximately 54–70 AU and 87–119 AU, with a gas ring-like enhancement between them (Favre et al. 2019).
These results show that AS 209 contains coordinated substructures in both dust and gas, consistent with strong dynamical perturbations within the disk.

The dust morphology is based on the 1.3 mm continuum measurements reported by Fedele et al. (2018), while the gas-related entries summarize the radial structure inferred from C\(^{18}\)O observations and thermo-chemical modeling in Favre et al. (2019). In this context, the gas gaps do not represent empty regions, but zones where the gas surface density is significantly reduced relative to neighboring annuli.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
Dust in protoplanetary disks emits thermal radiation at millimeter wavelengths, making it possible to map its radial distribution with interferometric observations. In AS 209, the 1.3 mm continuum reveals that the dust is not distributed smoothly, but instead concentrated into rings separated by narrow gaps (Fedele et al. 2018).
The outer dust ring at 130 AU is especially important because it is spatially associated with an outer gas ring seen in C\(^{18}\)O, suggesting that the dust and gas structures are physically connected (Favre et al. 2019). In addition, the narrow widths of the gaps imply that the dust distribution is being shaped by localized dynamical processes rather than by a simple monotonic radial decline.
The continuum morphology of AS 209 is therefore consistent with a disk in which solids are being redistributed by pressure variations and gas–dust interactions, likely in connection with the gravitational influence of one or more forming planets.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The disk around AS 209 also contains a structured molecular gas component that can be studied through line emission from CO, \(^{13}\)CO, C\(^{18}\)O, and DCO\(^+\) (Favre et al. 2019). These tracers probe different observational regimes, but the C\(^{18}\)O line is especially important because it is optically thin, allowing the gas surface density perturbations to be studied more directly.
The gas disk extends beyond the region traced by the millimeter dust continuum, with CO emission detected at radii greater than 200 AU (Favre et al. 2019). The gas also shows a rotational pattern consistent with Keplerian motion, and the disk-averaged spectra are centered around a systemic velocity of 4.5 km s\(^{-1}\) (Favre et al. 2019).
A key result of the gas analysis is that the C\(^{18}\)O radial profile exhibits a deficit between the two main dust gaps, while DCO\(^+\) shows the opposite trend, becoming enhanced in the region between them (Favre et al. 2019). This indicates that the gas disk is dynamically and chemically structured, rather than smooth.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
Several lines of evidence suggest that AS 209 is undergoing planet formation. The dust continuum exhibits narrow gaps and rings, while the gas observations show that these dust substructures are accompanied by real perturbations in the gas surface density (Fedele et al. 2018; Favre et al. 2019).

“Our findings confirm that the previously observed dust gaps are very likely due to perturbation of the gas surface density that is induced by a planet of at least 0.2 MJ in formation.”

Thermo-chemical modeling shows that the observed C\(^{18}\)O deficit between the two main dust gaps cannot be reproduced unless the gas surface density is reduced by a factor of roughly 5–10 within those regions (Favre et al. 2019). This means that the gaps are not merely features of the dust distribution, but correspond to actual perturbations in the gas disk.
Hydrodynamical simulations are consistent with this picture. In particular, models with a planet of 0.2–0.3 Jupiter masses located near 95–100 AU reproduce the depth and radial extent of the inferred gas perturbation (Favre et al. 2019). These simulations show that an embedded planet can open a gas gap, alter the dust-to-gas ratio, and create pressure structures that trap drifting dust grains in rings.
Taken together, the dust morphology, the gas surface density deficit, and the hydrodynamical modeling strongly support the interpretation that AS 209 hosts at least one forming giant planet in its outer disk.

title: AS 209 — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of AS 209
AS 209 is a particularly important disk because it links dust substructures, gas density perturbations, and planet–disk interaction models within the same system. The dust continuum shows clear rings and gaps, but the gas analysis demonstrates that these are not purely superficial dust features: the gas itself is depleted in the same broad radial zones (Favre et al. 2019).
This result is physically significant because planets are expected to perturb the surrounding gas through gravitational torques, opening gaps and redistributing material into adjacent rings. In AS 209, the observed combination of dust rings, gas depletion traced by C\(^{18}\)O, and DCO\(^+\) enhancement between the gaps is consistent with a disk in which a forming giant planet is actively modifying the local disk environment.
AS 209 therefore stands out as one of the best observational laboratories for studying how planet formation reshapes both the solid and gaseous components of a protoplanetary disk.

Clarke, Ilaria Pascucci, Agnes Kospal, et al. 2018. “ALMA Continuum Observations of the Protoplanetary Disk AS 209: Evidence of Multiple Gaps Opened by a Single Planet?” Astronomy & Astrophysics 610: A24. https://doi.org/10.1051/0004-6361/201731978.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Figure 1: Annotated ALMA image of the protoplanetary disk surrounding the young star HL Tau. The ring–gap structure visible in the dust emission traces variations in the distribution of solid material within the disk. Image credit: ALMA (ESO/NAOJ/NRAO).
As shown in Figure 1, the HL Tau disk exhibits a sequence of bright rings and dark gaps in the dust continuum emission.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Object names



Catalog
Name




Primary name
HL Tau


Other designations
IRAS 04368+2557


Region
Taurus Molecular Cloud

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Location
HL Tau is located in the Taurus Molecular Cloud, one of the closest star-forming regions to Earth. The system lies at a distance of approximately 140 parsecs (~450 light-years). Because of its proximity and young age, this region contains many protostars and protoplanetary disks that can be studied in detail.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Overview of the system
HL Tau is one of the most iconic protoplanetary disks ever observed. High-resolution observations obtained with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) revealed a complex system of bright rings and dark gaps within the disk surrounding this very young star (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015).
These structures suggest that planet formation may begin much earlier than previously thought. With an estimated age of about 1–2 million years, HL Tau has become a key laboratory for studying the early stages of protoplanetary disk evolution and planet formation.
Physical models of the disk also indicate that millimeter-sized dust grains are concentrated in a very thin layer near the midplane and that turbulence in the system is relatively weak (Pinte et al. 2016). These conditions favor dust growth and the early formation of planetesimals.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Disk morphology
ALMA observations show that the disk of HL Tau contains a complex radial structure composed of bright rings and dark gaps (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015). In total, 14 substructures have been identified, organized into seven bright rings (B1–B7) and seven dark gaps (D1–D7).
These features appear consistently across multiple millimeter wavelengths, confirming that they are real physical structures rather than observational artifacts.
The rings correspond to regions with a high concentration of dust, while the gaps indicate areas where the density of material is significantly reduced. This pattern suggests that strong dynamical processes are shaping the disk, possibly related to interactions between the disk and forming planets.

Bright ring

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Dust in the disk
Dust in protoplanetary disks emits thermal radiation at millimeter wavelengths, allowing astronomers to study its distribution using interferometric observations. The spectral index of the emission provides information about the size and properties of the dust grains (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015).
In HL Tau, the central regions and some bright rings appear optically thick, indicating a high concentration of solid material. In contrast, the dark gaps exhibit lower brightness and higher spectral index values, suggesting that these regions contain less dust.
The dust opacity index β ≈ 0.3–0.8 indicates that dust grains have grown significantly beyond the typical size of interstellar grains, an important step toward the formation of planetesimals (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015).
Disk models also show that millimeter-sized grains are strongly concentrated near the midplane, forming a geometrically thin layer. At a distance of about 100 AU, the vertical thickness of this dust layer is only ≈2 AU, indicating strong dust settling (Pinte et al. 2016).
This behavior implies that turbulence in the disk is relatively weak, with an estimated turbulent viscosity of α ≈ 3 × 10⁻⁴ (Pinte et al. 2016). Hydrodynamic simulations further show that small grains remain coupled to the gas, while larger millimeter grains migrate toward pressure maxima at the edges of gaps, where they accumulate into rings (Dipierro et al. 2015).

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Gas in the disk
The disk around HL Tau also contains a large amount of molecular gas, observable through spectral lines of molecules such as CO, HCO⁺, CN, and HCN (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015). These emission lines allow astronomers to measure the velocity of the gas through the Doppler effect.
Observations show that the gas follows a Keplerian rotation pattern around the central star, with velocities ranging from approximately 2 to 12 km/s. Position–velocity diagrams of CO emission indicate that the mass of the central star is close to 1.7 solar masses (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015).
The gas disk also extends farther than the region where millimeter dust emission is detected, which is typical in protoplanetary disks.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Evidence of planet formation
Several lines of evidence suggest that HL Tau may already be forming planets. The dark gaps observed in the disk correspond to regions where the dust density is significantly lower than in neighboring rings (ALMA Partnership et al. 2015).
Models indicate that the formation of the largest gaps may require the removal of up to ≈40 Earth masses of dust, material that could have been incorporated into growing planetary cores (Dipierro et al. 2015).
Hydrodynamic simulations show that embedded planets can generate these structures through gravitational perturbations. Models including three protoplanets located at approximately 13.2 AU, 32.3 AU, and 68.8 AU reproduce many of the observed features of the disk. The estimated masses of these planets are about 0.2, 0.27, and 0.55 Jupiter masses (Dipierro et al. 2015).
These simulations also show that gaps are easier to open in dust than in gas, meaning that the structures observed in millimeter continuum images primarily trace the distribution of solid material.

title: HL Tau — Protoplanetary Disk
text:
Physical interpretation of HL Tau
Taken together, high-resolution ALMA observations and physical models indicate that HL Tau is an extremely young system in which planet formation may already be underway. The presence of massive dust rings, deep gaps, and millimeter-sized grains suggests that solid material in the disk is evolving toward the formation of planetesimals. Hydrodynamic simulations indicate that several young protoplanets with masses comparable to giant planets could explain the observed disk structures (Dipierro et al. 2015).

Dipierro, Giovanni, Giuseppe Lodato, Leonardo Testi, et al. 2015. “Planet–Disk Interactions in the HL Tau Disk.” Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society Letters 453: L73–77. https://doi.org/10.1093/mnrasl/slv105.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
This Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog is an educational and scientific resource designed to explore the physical properties, morphologies, and evolutionary processes of protoplanetary disks.
The goal of this catalog is to organize observational and theoretical knowledge about young circumstellar disks using results from the scientific literature. Each disk entry summarizes its stellar properties, disk morphology, and observational signatures that may be related to planet formation.
The catalog focuses particularly on disk substructures, such as rings, gaps, spirals, and asymmetries, which have become central observational features in modern studies of planet-forming disks.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
section: Disk accretion and angular momentum transport
text:
Disk accretion and angular momentum transport
Gas in a disk orbits the central star approximately in Keplerian rotation. However, for material to accrete onto the star, it must lose angular momentum. This redistribution of angular momentum drives disk evolution.
Disk dynamics therefore play a central role in shaping disk structure. As Andrews (2020) notes:

“Disks are profoundly affected by their fluid dynamics.” (Andrews, 2020)

Theoretical models describe this evolution using the framework of thin accretion disks, where the vertical thickness of the disk is small compared with its radius. In fact:

“Protoplanetary disks are observed to be geometrically thin, in the sense that the vertical scale height (h r), and they typically have inferred masses (M_{disk} M_*). These properties imply that models of disk evolution should be grounded within the theory of thin accretion disks.” (Armitage, 2011)

Angular momentum transport may occur through several mechanisms, including turbulence, magnetic processes, and disk winds.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Gravitational instability
In sufficiently massive and cold disks, self-gravity can become important. In this regime, gravitational instabilities can generate spiral density patterns that redistribute mass and angular momentum within the disk.
These spiral structures may also concentrate solid particles and potentially contribute to the formation of planetesimals.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Rings and cavities
These systems display a central cavity surrounded by a bright ring of dust emission. This morphology is typical of transition disks.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Rings and gaps
This is the most common substructure pattern observed in millimeter continuum images. It consists of concentric bright rings separated by darker gaps.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Arcs
Some disks show asymmetric arc-shaped structures, which may correspond to vortices or local dust concentrations.

title: Pilot Protoplanetary Disk Catalog
text:
Spirals
Large-scale spiral patterns can arise from gravitational instability or perturbations produced by massive companions or forming planets.
